Table of contents

  • The naive failure: raw accuracy vs AoA
  • Fix 1: animacy-adjusted LWL difficulty
  • Fix 2: IRT difficulty instead of AoA
  • Fix 3: restrict to well-estimated words
  • Summary of correlations
  • Read

4. Aggregate item-level linking (the classic analysis, updated)

peekbank-items

This notebook reproduces the background failure (4A: naive word-level LWL accuracy correlates with Wordbank AoA in the wrong direction) on 2026.1 data, then tests whether the two methodological fixes help at the aggregate item level:

  1. a better LWL measure (animacy-adjusted random intercept vs raw accuracy);
  2. a better CDI measure (IRT difficulty b vs AoA).

No child information enters here — this is the “aggregate” data setting, and it is the first of the three settings (aggregate / imputed / precise) that notebook 5 compares in a common trial-level framework. This notebook does the aggregate case thoroughly at the word level; notebook 5 picks it up at the trial level and adds the child-specific settings.

What the measures are (all per target word):

  • avg_accuracy / avg_elogit — raw mean looking accuracy for the word, pooled over all trials. Badly confounded: a word’s average is dragged around by the ages and studies it happens to be tested in.
  • intercept_base — the word’s random intercept from a mixed model (notebook 2) that controls for age (within dataset), child, dataset, and the distractor.
  • intercept_adj — the same, but the model also removes target/distractor animacy (perceptual salience). This is our cleanest LWL difficulty measure: a word’s recognition ease with confounds partialled out. Higher = recognized better. (See 2_lwl_item_difficulty for the model.)

The CDI-side difficulty is the IRT b and Wordbank aoa from notebook 3.

Code 
library(here)
library(ggrepel)
library(patchwork)
source(here("helper", "common.R"))
knitr::opts_chunk$set(cache = FALSE)

lwl <- readRDS(here("cached_intermediates", "2_lwl_item.Rds"))
cdi <- readRDS(here("cached_intermediates", "3_cdi_difficulty.Rds"))

# CDI-side difficulty per word
cdi_diff <- cdi$item_pars |>
  select(item, a, b) |>
  left_join(cdi$aoas |> select(item, aoa), by = "item")

# LWL-side measures per word, merged with CDI difficulty
mdf <- lwl$lwl_word |>
  mutate(item = target_label) |>
  inner_join(cdi_diff, by = "item")

We have 121 words with both LWL and CDI measures.

The naive failure: raw accuracy vs AoA

Direction we want: earlier-acquired (low AoA) words should be recognized better (high accuracy) → a negative slope.

Code 
plot_corr <- function(data, x, y, xlab, ylab, min_n = 0) {
  data <- dplyr::filter(data, n_trials >= min_n)
  r <- cor(data[[x]], data[[y]], use = "complete.obs")
  ggplot(data, aes(x = .data[[x]], y = .data[[y]])) +
    geom_point(aes(size = n_trials), alpha = .4) +
    geom_smooth(method = "lm") +
    geom_text_repel(aes(label = target_label), size = 2.3, max.overlaps = 8) +
    xlab(xlab) + ylab(ylab) +
    labs(subtitle = sprintf("r = %.2f  (%d words)", r, nrow(data))) +
    theme(legend.position = "none")
}

plot_corr(mdf, "aoa", "avg_accuracy", "Wordbank AoA (months)", "Avg accuracy")

As in the background analysis, this goes the wrong way: later-acquired words show higher accuracy, driven by age confounding (easy words are tested in younger samples) and salience.

Fix 1: animacy-adjusted LWL difficulty

Code 
plot_corr(mdf, "aoa", "intercept_adj",
          "Wordbank AoA (months)", "LWL ease (animacy-adjusted intercept)")

Fix 2: IRT difficulty instead of AoA

Code 
plot_corr(mdf, "b", "intercept_adj",
          "CDI IRT difficulty (b)", "LWL ease (animacy-adjusted intercept)")

Fix 3: restrict to well-estimated words

The plots above pool all 121 words, but most have very few trials, so their intercept_adj is a noisy estimate. Noise in a predictor attenuates correlations toward zero (classic measurement-error attenuation). Restricting to words with a lot of data (here, >200 trials) gives each word a reliable difficulty estimate — and this is where the relationship becomes clear and points the right way (harder/later-learned word ⇒ lower LWL ease ⇒ negative slope).

Code 
library(patchwork)
p_aoa <- plot_corr(mdf, "aoa", "intercept_adj",
                   "Wordbank AoA (months)", "LWL ease (adj. intercept)", min_n = 200)
p_b <- plot_corr(mdf, "b", "intercept_adj",
                 "CDI IRT difficulty (b)", "LWL ease (adj. intercept)", min_n = 200)
p_aoa + p_b

It takes both moves. On the same well-estimated words, raw accuracy goes from the wrong-direction r ≈ +0.28 (all words) to roughly flat (r ≈ 0) — the frequency filter removes the confound but leaves no positive linkage — whereas the adjusted intercept reaches r ≈ −0.45. So the frequency filter cleans up the predictor and the LWL-side modeling supplies the actual signal.

Summary of correlations

All cells are correlations of an LWL measure with a CDI difficulty measure. Success = negative (harder/later word ⇒ lower LWL ease). The naive cell (top-left, avg_accuracy × aoa) is the one the background found positive.

Code 
lwl_measures <- c("avg_accuracy", "avg_elogit", "intercept_base", "intercept_adj")
cdi_measures <- c("aoa", "b")

grid <- expand_grid(lwl = lwl_measures, cdi = cdi_measures, hf_only = c(FALSE, TRUE))

results <- grid |>
  rowwise() |>
  mutate(
    r = {
      d <- if (hf_only) filter(mdf, n_trials > 200) else mdf
      cor(d[[lwl]], d[[cdi]], use = "complete.obs")
    },
    n_words = if (hf_only) sum(mdf$n_trials > 200) else nrow(mdf)
  ) |>
  ungroup()

results |>
  mutate(cell = paste0(cdi, ifelse(hf_only, " (n>200)", ""))) |>
  select(lwl, cell, r) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = cell, values_from = r) |>
  kable(digits = 2, caption = "Correlation of LWL measure (rows) with CDI difficulty (cols). Negative = success.")
Correlation of LWL measure (rows) with CDI difficulty (cols). Negative = success.
lwl aoa aoa (n>200) b b (n>200)
avg_accuracy 0.28 0.01 0.27 0.03
avg_elogit 0.29 0.00 0.28 0.01
intercept_base -0.11 -0.41 -0.11 -0.39
intercept_adj -0.09 -0.45 -0.09 -0.45

Read

The table makes the diagnosis concrete. Reading the intercept_adj row under the (n>200) columns: with a clean LWL measure and well-estimated words, the aggregate item-level correlation finally points the right way (≈ −0.45). The naive cell (avg_accuracy × aoa, all words) is the +0.28 failure the background reported. IRT b and aoa behave almost identically (they correlate ~0.99; see notebook 3), so the CDI-side measure is not the lever — the LWL-side modeling and the frequency filter are.

But even at its best, the aggregate level throws away the within-child structure: a child only “knows” a word probabilistically, and the 2AFC decision could depend on the distractor too. Notebook 5 carries this same aggregate analysis to the trial level and adds the child-specific settings (imputed and precise word knowledge) in one common framework; notebook 6 drills into the precise, within-child item-specific test.
